subject: Physics
solution:
For SHM with $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$, the potential energy is
$U = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x^2 = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 A^2 \sin^2(\omega t)$.
Maximum potential energy occurs when $\sin^2(\omega t) = 1$, i.e., when $\omega t = \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}, ...$
The first maximum occurs at $\omega t = \frac{\pi}{2}$, giving $t = \frac{\pi}{2\omega} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{T}{2\pi} = \frac{T}{4}$.
Since this is given as $t = \frac{T}{2\beta}$.
We have $\frac{T}{4} = \frac{T}{2\beta}$, so $\beta = 2$.

$$

subject: Physics
solution:
The position of a particle performing an SHM is given by:

x=A sin(ωt+ϕ)

Since at t=0 s, the particle is at x=0, therefore the initial phase ϕ=0.

At time t=2 s, the particle is at x=A2.

∴ A2=A sin2ω⇒ω=π12 s-1

Therefore, time period

T=2πω=24 s

The total time taken from x=0 to x=A is T4=6 s.

Since the time taken from x=0 to x=A2 is 2 s, therefore the time taken from x=A2 to x=A is 4 s.

For forced oscillations, the displacement is given by

subject: Physics
solution:
In a simple harmonic oscillator, if the displacement is $x = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$, the velocity is $v = A\omega \cos(\omega t + \phi)$.
The kinetic energy is given by $K = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = \frac{1}{2}m A^2 \omega^2 \cos^2(\omega t + \phi)$.
Using the identity $\cos^2 \theta = \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}$, we get $K = \frac{1}{4}m A^2 \omega^2 [1 + \cos(2\omega t + 2\phi)]$.
The angular frequency of the kinetic energy oscillation is $\omega_{KE} = 2\omega$, where $\omega$ is the angular frequency of the SHM.
Given $\omega_{KE} = 176$ rad/s, we have $2\omega = 176$, which gives $\omega = 88$ rad/s.
The frequency of the simple harmonic oscillator is $f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$.
Substituting the values: $f = \frac{88}{2 \times \frac{22}{7}} = \frac{88 \times 7}{44} = 2 \times 7 = 14$ Hz.

subject: Physics
solution:
Let $T_1$ and $T_2$ be the time period of the two pendulums $\mathrm{T}_1=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{\mathrm{~g}}}$ and $\mathrm{T}_2=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{4}{\mathrm{~g}}}$ As $\ell_1 < \ell_2$ therefore $\mathrm{T}_1 < \mathrm{T}_2$ Let at $\mathrm{t}=0$ they start swinging together. Since their time periods are different, the swinging will not be in unison always. Only when number of completed oscillations
differ by an integer, the two pendulums will again begin to swing together Let longer length pendulum complete $\mathrm{n}$ oscillation and shorter length pendulum complete $(n+1)$ oscillation. For unison swinging
$$
\begin{aligned}
& (n+1) T_1=n_2 \\
& (n+1) \times 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{g}}=(n) \times 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{4}{g}} \\
& \Rightarrow n=1 \\
& \therefore n+1=1+1=2
\end{aligned}
$$

As can be seen from the diagram, the initial phase of the particle is given by

φ=30°= π6

The equation of motion of the particle at its initial position at time t=0 can be written as

xt=rcosθ   ...(1)

where, θ is the angular displacement of the particle at time t=t.

Since, the position of the particle at a later instant of time always lags behind its initial position at time t=0, the equation of motion of the particle at any instant of time is given by

 xt=rcosθ+φ   ...2

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (2) to obtain the required expression

x(t)=r cosωt+π6

subject: Physics
solution: $\begin{aligned} f & =\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{k_1+k_2}{m}} \\ f^{\prime} & =\frac{1}{2 \pi} 2 \sqrt{\frac{k_1+k_2}{m}}=2 f\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
The frequency of oscillation of a spring-mass system is given by $v = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$, where $k$ is the spring constant and $m$ is the mass.

The spring constant $k$ is inversely proportional to the length of the spring $L$, so $k \propto \dfrac{1}{L}$.

When the length of the spring is cut to half, the new length is $L' = \dfrac{L}{2}$. The new spring constant becomes $k' = 2k$.

The new frequency of oscillation is $v_2 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k'}{m}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{2k}{m}} = \sqrt{2} v_1$.

Therefore, the ratio $\dfrac{v_2}{v_1} = \sqrt{2}$.

Answer: $\sqrt{2}$
